nativeRange.habitat: Still and slow-moving waters: ponds, lakes, streams, ditches, marshes, and backwaters; highly tolerant of low oxygen, turbidity, and a wide temperature range

decorPreferences: flat stones or PVC tubes as spawning sites for males
live or artificial plants for cover

diet.notes: The Rosy Red Minnow is an undemanding omnivore that accepts a wide range of foods, including high-quality flake, micro-pellets, spirulina-based foods, algae wafers, and frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. It will also graze on algae and biofilm that develop naturally in an established tank. A varied diet with a plant-based component is generally recommended to support coloration and overall health in the rosy-red color morph.

goodWith: other cool-water minnows (e.g., white cloud mountain minnow)
smaller goldfish (fancy goldfish with similar temperature needs)
avoidWith: large predatory fish that can swallow them whole

imageCandidates.notes: Wild-type fathead minnow (Pimephales promelas); shows species body shape clearly. Rosy-red color morph images also available on Wikimedia.
FishBase photo gallery for Pimephales promelas; includes multiple wild specimens.

summary: The Rosy Red Minnow is a captive-bred orange-pink color morph of the Fathead Minnow (Pimephales promelas), native across North America. It tolerates temperatures from 10 to 26°C, making it well suited to unheated tanks and outdoor ponds. Often sold as feeder fish, it is increasingly kept as an aquarium species in its own right.
careNotes:
The Rosy Red Minnow is a selectively bred color morph of the native North American Fathead Minnow (Pimephales promelas), and the two are identical in care requirements. Among the more cold-tolerant aquarium fish available, it thrives in unheated tanks at room temperature (around 18–22°C) and tolerates temperatures as low as 10°C, making it well suited to cool-water setups alongside White Cloud Mountain Minnows, goldfish, or hillstream loaches. It also handles warmer conditions up to about 26°C, giving it considerable flexibility compared to many temperate species.

Fathead Minnows are broadly considered forgiving of water quality lapses, including brief low-oxygen events — a trait that has made them a standard species in scientific ecotoxicology studies, where researchers need animals that respond predictably to environmental stressors. Despite this resilience, stable water conditions and routine maintenance support the best coloration and lifespan.

The species is placed in family Leuciscidae (previously grouped within the broader Cyprinidae, before Leuciscidae was elevated to family rank in 2018). A group of at least 6 individuals is recommended; males can become mildly territorial around spawning sites but are generally not a threat to other tank inhabitants. Males are noticeably larger than females, develop small tubercles (rough bumps) on the snout during breeding season, and produce a mucus secretion on the crown of the head that they use to tend eggs deposited on the underside of flat surfaces. Flat rocks, broad leaves, or short lengths of PVC pipe make suitable spawning sites.

Rosy Red color forms may be slightly less robust than wild-type Fathead Minnows as a result of selective breeding. They are best avoided with large predatory fish or with purely tropical species that require temperatures above 28°C on a sustained basis.
breedingNotes: The Rosy Red Minnow is among the easier freshwater fish to breed in a home aquarium. Males establish territories around a flat surface — the underside of a stone, piece of slate, or PVC tube — and entice females to deposit adhesive eggs there. The male guards and fans the clutch until hatching, which typically takes 4–7 days depending on water temperature. Fry become free-swimming within a day of hatching and accept finely crushed flake food and infusoria. Multiple clutches can be produced from a single group within one season. Breeding is naturally triggered by rising temperatures in spring and can be induced in the aquarium by gradually raising the temperature from around 15°C to around 22°C.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rosy Red Minnow / Fathead Minnow
primary.notes: Primary hobbyist-trade source. Covers care, temperature range (50–75°F / 10–24°C), tank size, diet, compatibility, and notes on the rosy-red as a color morph of P. promelas. Also discusses feeder-fish origin and suitability for unheated tanks.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Pimephales promelas
additional.notes: Detailed species account. Confirms adult size (up to 9 cm TL for males), temperature (10–25°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness, native range across most of North America, breeding behaviour (egg-scatter on underside of substrate with male guarding), and taxonomy (Leuciscidae, Cypriniformes). Notes widespread use in ecotoxicology as a standard test organism.

additional.name: FishBase — Pimephales promelas
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 9.9 cm TL, commonly 6–8 cm), temperature range (4–25°C wild), native range (central North America from Canada to northern Mexico), diet (omnivore: algae, detritus, small invertebrates), and family placement (Leuciscidae). Also confirms status as a principal EPA ecotoxicology test species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Cool-water fish for unheated aquariums
additional.notes: Discusses Fathead / Rosy Red Minnow as an underrated unheated-tank option; confirms peaceful temperament, schooling behavior, ease of breeding, and broad temperature tolerance (similar to white cloud minnows). Recommends groups of 6+.

additional.name: Tan M. & Armbruster J.W. (2018) — Revised nomenclature for North American leuciscids (Cypriniformes: Cyprinidae: Leuciscidae), Zootaxa 4495(1):139–147
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed paper establishing Leuciscidae as a full family elevated from Cyprinidae, directly affecting the family placement of Pimephales promelas. Cited to document the 2018 reclassification referenced in the species brief.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard eggs deposited on the underside of a flat surface such as a stone or PVC tube. No specialized equipment is needed to trigger spawning. Fry accept finely crushed flake food from the free-swimming stage onward, and multiple clutches per season are commonly observed.
